Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

It isn't clear to me why this is being changed from LGPL to GPL, since this is used by userland tools?  It is fine to include LGPL code into GPL programs like the kernel and non-GPL programs, but it isn't OK to include GPL code into non-GPL programs.

Also, please do not remove the Intel Copyright statement from the header.

Patch Set 1:

This header is also used by kernel space as well.

Patch Set 1:

It is fine to use the LGPL v2.1 code in a GPL v2.0-only program.  LGPL v2.1 is compatible with GPL v2.0, see:

 http://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#AllCompatibility
 http://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#compat-matrix-footnote-7

What we can't do is change this header to be GPLv2.0 only and then use it in a non-GPL userspace program, so it needs to remain as LGPLv2.1.
Uploaded patch set 2.

Patch Set 2:

Build Started https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/36458/

Patch Set 2:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review-1

It is ok to change the license on lib-dlc.h since it was written by Amir, and only modified by James since then. However, modifying uapi_kernelcomm.h should be done in a separate patch since it was written originally at Sun/Oracle and needs a signoff by Seagate, just like liblustreapi.[ch] and related files. 

I'm not sure if the header cleanup is far enough along that we might attempt such a mass license change without dragging in unrelated code, but at lease lib-dlc.h can be fixed in this patch.

Patch Set 2:

I can remove the license change in uapi_kernelcomm.h. Just be aware currently the HSM code is technically using the header illegally.

Patch Set 2:

Also Frank Zago is main author of that header as well which was created from the split.

Patch Set 2: Verified+1

Build Successful 
 
https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/36458/ : SUCCESS
Uploaded patch set 3.

Patch Set 3:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 2:

I don't think there is anything "illegal" about the usage, it just means that the combined GPL+LGPL work would be GPL until there are no more GPL licensed files remaining (i.e. liblustreapi is also fixed). 

If the uapi header is derived from a GPL file, then it needs to stay GPL, even if Frank created the file originally. If it was not derived from an existing GPL file, then it wouldn't have included all of the Oracle copyright baggage as well, so I suspect the best way forward would be for Cray to discuss the LU-5969 license change with Seagate again.

commit.subject: LU-6613 lnet: change lib-dlc.h to LGPLv2.1 license
commit.message:
LU-6613 lnet: change lib-dlc.h to LGPLv2.1 license

Change the lib-dlc.h header to the proper LGPLv2.1 license

Change-Id: Ic152d0722ec775d57cf7702058e6772ce2f5ff8a
Signed-off-by: James Simmons <uja.ornl@yahoo.com>
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/17697
Tested-by: Jenkins
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: John L. Hammond <john.hammond@intel.com>
